display_name: Pacific Northwest Agricultural Region (Oregon and Washington)

neighborhoods.name: Willamette Valley, Oregon (Berry and Hop Country)
primary_groups: Mexican American families from Texas and the Southwest
neighborhoods.character: The Willamette Valley stretches from Portland south to Eugene — a green, rainy corridor between the Cascades and the Coast Range that produces berries, hops, nursery stock, and vegetables. Mexican workers arrived during WWII to fill the labor vacuum left by Japanese American incarceration and military enlistment. The work was seasonal — berry picking in summer, hop harvesting in fall — and the rain-soaked valley was a shock to workers from the arid Southwest. Small towns like Woodburn, Independence, and Salem became the centers of permanent Mexican settlement.
key_institutions: Seasonal labor camps near Independence and Salem
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: The Willamette Valley was as far from the Mexican border as the bracero program reached — if {ancestor_name} picked berries in the rain near Woodburn, the green hills and cold mornings were nothing like home, and the distance from family in Mexico or Texas was measured in days of travel, not hours.

neighborhoods.name: Yakima Valley, Washington (Apple and Hop Country)
neighborhoods.character: The Yakima Valley in central Washington was — and remains — one of the most productive agricultural regions in the Pacific Northwest. Irrigated by the Yakima River, the valley produced apples, hops, asparagus, and sugar beets. Before the war, Japanese American families were prominent farmers. Their forced removal in 1942 created an immediate labor crisis that bracero workers were recruited to fill. The towns of Yakima, Toppenish, Sunnyside, and Grandview became bracero destinations. The valley is also Yakama Nation homeland, adding another layer to the labor landscape.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: The Yakima Valley's bracero workers came because Japanese American farmers were taken away — if {ancestor_name} was sent to the valley during the war, they filled a labor gap created by one of the great injustices of American history, picking apples in orchards that incarcerated families had planted.

neighborhoods.name: Walla Walla, Washington
neighborhoods.character: Walla Walla in southeastern Washington was wheat, onion, and sugar beet country — drier and more isolated than the Yakima or Willamette valleys. Bracero workers were recruited for sugar beet cultivation (backbreaking hand labor) and wheat harvest support. The town's Mexican community grew slowly from bracero-era roots into a permanent settlement. The isolation of southeastern Washington meant workers were far from any Mexican American community of significant size — the nearest large barrio was in the Yakima Valley, hours away.
key_institutions: Walla Walla sugar beet processing plant
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Walla Walla was the far edge of the bracero world — if {ancestor_name} worked sugar beets in southeastern Washington, they were as isolated from the Mexican American community as it was possible to be in the continental United States, harvesting root vegetables in country that looked nothing like home.

neighborhoods.name: Hood River, Oregon (Fruit Orchards)
primary_groups: Japanese American orchard families (pre-1942, some returned post-war)
neighborhoods.character: The Hood River Valley, east of Portland in the Columbia River Gorge, was premium apple and pear country — the volcanic soil and mountain water produced fruit that commanded top prices. Japanese American families had been the backbone of Hood River orcharding until the 1942 removal. The community's return after the war was met with organized hostility — Hood River was one of the most openly anti-Japanese communities in Oregon. Bracero workers and Mexican American families filled the labor gap during and after the war.
key_institutions: Labor camps in the Hood River Valley
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Hood River's orchards were beautiful and the welcome was cold — if {ancestor_name} picked apples there, they worked trees that Japanese American families had planted and been forced to abandon, in a valley where the growers needed the labor but the town made clear that outsiders were tolerated, not accepted.

neighborhoods.name: Woodburn, Oregon (Permanent Settlement)
primary_groups: Mexican American families (settled from bracero-era migration)
neighborhoods.character: Woodburn, a small town in the northern Willamette Valley, became the first permanent Mexican American community in Oregon. Families who had come as bracero workers or as migrant farm labor settled year-round, building a community from scratch in a place that had no Mexican institutions, no Spanish-language services, and no precedent for their presence. By the 1960s, Woodburn's Mexican American community was large enough to sustain its own businesses, churches, and social organizations — a permanent outpost far from the border.
key_institutions: PCUN (Pineros y Campesinos Unidos del Noroeste — farmworker union, founded 1985)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Woodburn was where the migrant trail ended for some families — if {ancestor_name} settled there, they chose to stay in a small Oregon town a thousand miles from the border, building a Mexican American community from nothing in a place where they were the first generation to speak Spanish on Main Street.

major_employers.name: Berry Farms (Willamette Valley)
major_employers.wages: Piece rates by the flat or pound — strawberry pickers earned $0.08-0.15 per pound (1940s-1950s). A fast picker could earn $4-8/day. Cannery workers earned $0.65-0.90/hr. The season was short — June through September — meaning workers needed to follow other crops before and after.
major_employers.working_conditions: Berry picking in the Willamette Valley meant working in wet conditions — morning dew soaked workers by dawn, and the Oregon rain could start at any time. The work was stooped and repetitive. Unlike California agriculture, the Pacific Northwest berry season was compressed — workers had to earn a season's wages in three to four months. Housing was rudimentary — seasonal camps with bunkhouses or tents, often without heat despite the cold Pacific Northwest mornings.
major_employers.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} picked strawberries in the Willamette Valley, the pay was by the flat and the mornings were wet — a worker from the Southwest learned fast that Oregon sunshine was not guaranteed, and that a good picking day could turn into a lost day with one rainstorm.

major_employers.name: Apple Orchards (Yakima Valley, Hood River)
major_employers.wages: Piece rates by the bin — apple pickers earned $0.50-1.00 per bin (1940s-1950s), with skilled workers filling 8-15 bins per day for $4-10/day. Packing house workers earned $0.65-0.90/hr. Apple harvest season ran September through November, making it a late-season complement to summer berry work.
major_employers.working_conditions: Apple picking required climbing ladders carrying heavy canvas bags, reaching into trees, and moving ladders between rows. Falls were the leading cause of injury. The Yakima Valley's continental climate meant cold mornings in October — frost on the ladders was a hazard workers from warmer climates had never encountered. Orchards that had been tended by Japanese American families before 1942 were maintained by bracero labor during and after the war.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The apple harvest came at the end of the season when mornings were cold and the ladders were slick with frost — if {ancestor_name} picked Yakima apples, they may have worked orchards planted by Japanese American families who had been taken away, filling a labor gap that one injustice created and another exploitation sustained.

major_employers.name: Hop Farms
major_employers.wages: Piece rates by the box — hop pickers earned $0.04-0.08 per pound (1940s). The hop harvest was a major seasonal event in the Willamette and Yakima valleys. Before mechanization, thousands of workers were needed for the September harvest. Hop picking had historically employed Native American, Chinese, Japanese, and Anglo workers before Mexican labor became dominant during WWII.
major_employers.working_conditions: Hop vines grow on tall trellises — pre-mechanization harvest involved pulling down the vines and stripping the cones by hand. Workers were covered in hop resin, which caused skin irritation. The hop harvest was a communal event that drew families and communities to the fields. Hop kilns (oast houses) for drying the hops were distinctive landmarks. The introduction of mechanical harvesters in the 1950s-1960s dramatically reduced labor needs.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Hops were the Pacific Northwest's cash crop before wine — if {ancestor_name} stripped hop vines in the Yakima or Willamette Valley, they were part of a harvest tradition that had drawn workers from every background, from Native pickers to Chinese laborers to braceros, each generation replacing the last.

major_employers.name: Sugar Beet Processing (Yakima, Walla Walla, Willamette)
major_employers.wages: Sugar beet work was paid by the acre for field work ($15-25/acre for thinning, 1940s) and by the ton for harvest. Processing plant workers earned $0.65-1.00/hr. Beet field work was considered the most backbreaking agricultural labor in the Pacific Northwest.
major_employers.working_conditions: Sugar beet cultivation required intensive hand labor — thinning the seedlings in spring (crawling on hands and knees to remove excess plants), weeding through summer, and topping the beets at harvest (cutting the greens off with a heavy knife). The work was so physically demanding that most Anglo workers refused it, creating the labor demand that brought bracero workers to the Pacific Northwest. The sugar companies (Utah-Idaho Sugar, Amalgamated Sugar) recruited workers directly from Mexico.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Sugar beet work was what brought {ancestor_name} to the Pacific Northwest — the sugar companies recruited from Mexico because no one else would crawl down beet rows on hands and knees, thinning seedlings in cold spring mud for wages paid by the acre.

major_employers.name: Railroad Section Work (Northern Pacific, Great Northern, Union Pacific)
major_employers.wages: Section hands earned $1.50-2.50/day (1920s-1930s), rising to $0.75-1.00/hr (1940s-1950s). Railroad work was year-round, providing stability that agricultural work could not. A separate bracero railroad program operated from 1943-1945, bringing Mexican workers to maintain Pacific Northwest rail lines.
major_employers.working_conditions: The northern railroads relied on Mexican section gangs for track maintenance across Oregon and Washington. Unlike the Southwest, Pacific Northwest railroad work meant cold winters, rain, and isolation — section camps in the Cascades or eastern Oregon were far from any community. But the work was steady, and railroad section camps sometimes became the nucleus of permanent Mexican communities when workers settled at their section points.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The railroad was year-round work in a region where farming was seasonal — if {ancestor_name} maintained track for the Northern Pacific, the section camp in the Cascades or along the Columbia was home, and the steady paycheck was worth the rain and isolation.

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Bracero workers were housed in labor camps operated by growers or growers' associations — barracks or bunkhouses near the fields. Migrant families who were not in the bracero program (which recruited only men) lived in seasonal camps, tents, or auto courts. Railroad section workers lived in section houses provided by the railroad. The Pacific Northwest's agricultural housing was generally worse than California's because the shorter season meant less investment in worker facilities.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: The Pacific Northwest had no established Mexican American community before WWII — bracero workers arrived in a region with no barrios, no Spanish-language institutions, and no cultural infrastructure. Initial settlement was entirely tied to the workplace — camps at farms, section houses at railroads. Permanent Mexican communities emerged slowly in Woodburn OR, Yakima Valley WA, and the Tri-Cities WA as families who had been seasonal workers chose to stay year-round.
housing_patterns.trajectory: Labor camp (seasonal) to year-round rental in a small town (for families choosing to settle) to owned house (10-20 years). The trajectory was slower than in the Southwest because the Pacific Northwest offered fewer support systems for Mexican families — no established barrios, no nearby border, no large Spanish-speaking community. Families who settled permanently were building communities from scratch, a generation behind the established Mexican American neighborhoods of Texas and California.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: The Pacific Northwest's treatment of Mexican agricultural workers was less violently hostile than the Southwest but still exploitative. The region's primary racial conflict before WWII was with Asian immigrants (Chinese exclusion, Japanese American incarceration) rather than Mexican workers. Bracero workers arrived into a racial landscape shaped by anti-Asian sentiment and were treated as temporary labor — welcomed for the harvest, expected to disappear afterward. Isolation and distance from any Mexican American community compounded the vulnerability.
specific_incidents: Japanese American incarceration (1942) — the forced removal of Japanese farmers from the Yakima Valley, Hood River, and Willamette Valley created the labor vacuum that bracero workers filled
Hood River anti-Japanese hostility (1945) — the American Legion removed Japanese American veterans' names from the honor roll, and the community organized against the return of Japanese families
Exploitation of bracero workers — wage theft, substandard housing, and contract violations were widespread but less documented than in California because the workforce was smaller and more isolated
Oregon's historical exclusion clause (1857 state constitution barred Black settlement) reflects the region's broader racial hostility toward non-white residents
nativist_organizations: Oregon and Washington growers' associations (controlled labor supply and resisted worker organization)
Historical legacy of anti-Chinese leagues and Japanese exclusion organizations

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Catholic Parishes (Valley Towns)
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Mexican and Mexican American farm workers and settled families
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Baptism, marriage, and burial records. Catholic parishes in Woodburn, Yakima, Toppenish, and other agricultural towns gradually added Spanish-language services as the Mexican community grew. Parish records may be the earliest institutional documentation of Mexican families in the Pacific Northwest.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The Catholic parish was the first institution to acknowledge that {ancestor_name}'s family was not just passing through — when a valley church started offering Mass in Spanish, it was a sign that the Mexican community had become permanent, and the parish records mark the transition from bracero camp to settled neighborhood.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Oregon Council of Churches / Migrant Ministry
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Migrant farm workers of all backgrounds
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Ministry reports, case files, advocacy records. The migrant ministry provided social services, education, and advocacy for farm workers in the Willamette Valley. Ministry records document working and living conditions and sometimes contain individual worker and family information.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The migrant ministry was often the only non-employer institution that bracero workers encountered in Oregon — if {ancestor_name} received services from the ministry, the case records may document their situation in a way that no grower or government record bothered to.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: PCUN (Pineros y Campesinos Unidos del Noroeste)
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Farm workers in Oregon — tree planters and agricultural workers
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Union membership records, organizing records. PCUN was founded by the children and grandchildren of bracero-era workers — the first farmworker union in Oregon. While post-bracero era, PCUN's institutional memory and records connect to the earlier generation of workers.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: PCUN was built by the generation that stayed — if {ancestor_name}'s family settled in the Willamette Valley, their children and grandchildren may have been part of the farmworker organizing movement that finally gave voice to a community that had been working Oregon fields for decades in silence.

source_references.author: Erasmo Gamboa
source_references.title: Mexican Labor and World War II: Braceros in the Pacific Northwest, 1942-1947
source_references.notes: The definitive study of bracero labor in Oregon and Washington — covers recruitment, transport, working conditions, camp life, and the establishment of Mexican communities in the Pacific Northwest. Essential and irreplaceable for any PNW bracero narrative.

source_references.author: Jerry Garcia
source_references.title: Looking Like the Enemy: Japanese Mexicans, the Mexican State, and US Hegemony, 1897-1945
source_references.notes: Examines the intersection of Japanese and Mexican labor histories in the Pacific region, including the labor dynamics that connected Japanese American incarceration to bracero recruitment.

source_references.author: Gilberto Garcia
source_references.title: Mexican Americans in Oregon's Willamette Valley, 1940s-1990s
source_references.notes: Published in the Oregon Historical Quarterly. Documents the growth of Mexican American communities in the Willamette Valley from bracero-era origins through permanent settlement. One of the few scholarly works focused specifically on Oregon's Mexican American community.

source_references.author: Lauren Kessler
source_references.title: Stubborn Twig: Three Generations in the Life of a Japanese American Family
source_references.notes: Documents the Japanese American farming experience in Hood River, Oregon — the community whose displacement created the labor vacuum that bracero workers filled. Essential context for understanding the Pacific Northwest agricultural labor sequence.
